title: Submodules, Nested Repositories, and Dependency Boundaries · Graph Lens: Gita Submodule Change Publication
summary: A product pins three component repositories, one URL moved, a fork rewrites a dependency, recursive checkout executes untrusted configuration, and release builds disagree on nested state. Planned artifact: Add and verify the gita submodule change publication increment in submodule dependency and trust manifest. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Stage gitlink changes, publish dependency commits before superproject refs, review nested diffs, and verify that every referenced object is reachable.
Predict init, update, sync, set-url, set-branch, recursive, remote, merge, rebase, and shallow behavior across configuration layers.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-advanced cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Predict Gita Submodule Change Publication
steps.instruction:
theory case — Case G23 is a theory in a disposable repository. the release mismatch comes from an uninitialized nested gitlink rather than branch drift. The learner must predict prefixes and object IDs expose initialized, mismatched, and conflicted nested states, run git submodule status --recursive, explain the parser submodule is uninitialized and its recorded gitlink names c0ffee0, test this changed condition: make the referenced dependency commit unreachable remotely and fail the release transfer gate, and preserve this recovery boundary: archive superproject and reachable dependency refs before URL or gitlink migration.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Gita Submodule Change Publication contract for the supplied data.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Locate repository and operation state, compare working tree, index, refs, and graph, preserve a recovery reference, preview the smallest change, then verify both tree behavior and history topology.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Case G23 is a worked example in a disposable repository. the release mismatch comes from an uninitialized nested gitlink rather than branch drift. The learner must predict prefixes and object IDs expose initialized, mismatched, and conflicted nested states, run git submodule status --recursive, explain the parser submodule is uninitialized and its recorded gitlink names c0ffee0, test this changed condition: make the referenced dependency commit unreachable remotely and fail the release transfer gate, and preserve this recovery boundary: archive superproject and reachable dependency refs before URL or gitlink migration. A defensible solution applies Gita Submodule Change Publication, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to submodule dependency and trust manifest.

content.code:
# Advanced Git 2.55 deterministic scenario 2
# before-ref: main=a11ce00 candidate=b00b1e5
# hypothesis: the release mismatch comes from an uninitialized nested gitlink rather than branch drift
git submodule status --recursive
# expected: prefixes and object IDs expose initialized, mismatched, and conflicted nested states
# observation: the parser submodule is uninitialized and its recorded gitlink names c0ffee0
# changed-case: make the referenced dependency commit unreachable remotely and fail the release transfer gate
# after-ref: main=a11ce00 candidate=b00b1e5
# tree-proof: compare the exact affected tree and paths before judging success
# behavior-proof: retain a changed-case test or invariant that can falsify the Git-only conclusion
# recovery: archive superproject and reachable dependency refs before URL or gitlink migration
# transfer-limit: never execute untrusted nested hooks, builds, or recursive commands outside isolation
content.caption: Runnable repository-state trace example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

steps.title: Build Gita Submodule Change Publication evidence
steps.instruction: Append an Advanced Git evidence block headed "# Evidence: gita-submodule-change-publication-z6ro4m" with exact before and after refs; a falsifiable prediction; the bounded simulator command; expected, observed, tree, behavior, changed-case, and recovery evidence; and one honest real-repository transfer limit.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Gita Submodule Change Publication into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: gita-submodule-change-publication-z6ro4m
# scenario-key: trace-z6ro4m probe-m4or6z boundary-ro4mz6 owner-z6ro4mx fault-z6ro4mz
# before-ref: main=a11cez6 candidate=b00b14m
# hypothesis: the release mismatch comes from an uninitialized nested gitlink rather than branch drift
git submodule status --recursive
# expected: prefixes and object IDs expose initialized, mismatched, and conflicted nested states
# observation: the parser submodule is uninitialized and its recorded gitlink names c0ffee0
# changed-case: make the referenced dependency commit unreachable remotely and fail the release transfer gate
# after-ref: main=a11cez6 candidate=c0ffeem
# tree-proof: the affected tree and path set match the predicted repository transition
# behavior-proof: a changed input preserves the named product or release invariant
# recovery: archive superproject and reachable dependency refs before URL or gitlink migration
# transfer-limit: never execute untrusted nested hooks, builds, or recursive commands outside isolation
